The classification header has already been emitted. Do NOT emit it again. This skill handles **execution only** for STANDARD and STRICT tier tasks.\n>\n> If for any reason no classification header was emitted before this skill was invoked, emit one now using ONLY the tier values STRICT, STANDARD, LIGHT, or EXEMPT (no other values are valid).\n\n# /sc:task-unified - Unified Task Execution with Compliance\n\n## Purpose\n\nUnified command that merges orchestration capabilities with MCP compliance enforcement. Automatically classifies tasks into compliance tiers and enforces appropriate verification.\n\n**Key Benefits**:\n- Single command replaces confusion between `sc:task` and `sc:task-mcp`\n- Automatic tier classification with confidence scoring\n- Appropriate verification for each task type\n- Prevents over-engineering for trivial changes\n- Ensures safety for critical changes\n\n## Triggers\n\nUse `/sc:task` when:\n- Task involves code modifications with downstream impacts\n- Complexity score >0.6 with code modifications\n- Multi-file scope (>2 estimated affected files)\n- Security domain paths detected (auth/, security/, crypto/)\n- Refactoring or system-wide changes requested\n\n**Auto-Suggest Keywords**:\n- High confidence: \"implement feature\", \"refactor system\", \"fix security\", \"add authentication\", \"update database schema\"\n- Moderate confidence: \"add new\", \"create component\", \"update service\", \"modify API\"\n\n## Usage\n\n```bash\n/sc:task [description]                           # Auto-detect all dimensions\n/sc:task [description] --compliance strict       # Force STRICT tier\n/sc:task [description] --compliance light        # Force LIGHT tier\n/sc:task [description] --skip-compliance         # Bypass compliance (escape hatch)\n/sc:task [description] --verify auto             # Auto-select verification\n```\n\n## Behavioral Flow\n\n### 0. Classification (Already Performed)\n\nClassification was handled by the `/sc:task` command before this skill was invoked. The tier has been determined and the classification header has been emitted. Proceed directly to execution based on the classified tier.\n\n**Reference** — the tier keyword tables (for context only, do not re-classify):\n- **STRICT**: security, authentication, database, migration, refactor, breaking change, encrypt, token, session, oauth\n- **EXEMPT**: explain, search, commit, push, plan, discuss, brainstorm\n- **LIGHT**: typo, comment, whitespace, lint, docstring, formatting, minor\n- **STANDARD**: implement, add, create, update, fix, build, modify, change (default)\n\n### 2. Confidence Display (Human-Readable)\n\nAfter the mandatory classification header, show a human-readable summary:\n\n```\n**Tier: STANDARD** [████████░░] 80%\n\nClassified as STANDARD:\n- Keywords matched: add, implement\n- Confidence score: 0.78\n- Considered alternatives: STRICT (0.35)\n```\n\nIf confidence <70%, add prompt: \"⚠️ Low confidence. Override with: `--compliance [strict|standard|light|exempt]`\"\n\n**Note**: The machine-readable header (Section 0) is for telemetry/A/B testing. This human-readable display is for user understanding.\n\n### 3. Execution Phase\n\nExecute task according to tier requirements:\n\n**STRICT Execution**:\n1. Activate project (mcp__serena__activate_project)\n2. Verify git working directory clean (git status)\n3. Load codebase context (codebase-retrieval)\n4. Check relevant memories (list_memories -> read_memory)\n5. Identify all affected files and test files\n6. Make changes with full checklist\n7. Identify all files that import changed code\n8. Update all affected files\n9. Spawn verification agent (quality-engineer)\n10. Run comprehensive tests: `pytest [path] -v`\n11. Answer adversarial questions\n\n**STANDARD Execution**:\n1. Load context via codebase-retrieval\n2. Search downstream impacts (find_referencing_symbols OR grep)\n3. Make changes\n4. Run affected tests OR document manual verification\n5. Verify basic functionality\n\n**LIGHT Execution**:\n1. Quick scope check (files/lines within bounds)\n2. Make changes\n3. Quick sanity check (syntax valid, no obvious errors)\n4. Proceed with judgment\n\n**EXEMPT Execution**:\n1. Execute immediately\n2. No verification overhead\n\n### 4. Verification Phase\n\nRoute to appropriate verification based on tier and paths:\n\n| Compliance Tier | Verification Method | Token Cost | Timeout |\n|-----------------|---------------------|------------|---------|\n| STRICT | Sub-agent (quality-engineer) | 3-5K | 60s |\n| STANDARD | Direct test execution | 300-500 | 30s |\n| LIGHT | Skip verification | 0 | 0s |\n| EXEMPT | Skip verification | 0 | 0s |\n\n**Critical Path Override**: Paths matching `auth/`, `security/`, `crypto/`, `models/`, `migrations/` always trigger CRITICAL verification regardless of compliance tier.\n\n**Trivial Path Override**: Paths matching `*.md`, `docs/`, `*test*.py` may skip verification.\n\n### 5. Feedback Collection\n\nAfter completion, collect implicit feedback:\n- Track if user overrode tier (implicit classification feedback)\n- Note smooth completion vs errors (quality signal)\n- Store for calibration learning\n\n## MCP Integration\n\n**Required Servers by Tier**:\n- STRICT: Sequential, Serena (fallback not allowed)\n- STANDARD: Sequential, Context7 (fallback allowed)\n- LIGHT: None required (fallback allowed)\n- EXEMPT: None required\n\n**Circuit Breaker Behavior**:\n- If required servers unavailable for STRICT tier, block task execution\n- For other tiers, use fallbacks with noted limitations\n\n## Tool Coordination\n\n**Planning Phase**:\n1. TodoWrite: Create task breakdown\n2. codebase-retrieval: Load context\n3. list_memories / read_memory: Check project state\n\n**Execution Phase**:\n1. Edit/MultiEdit/Write: Make changes\n2. Grep/Glob: Find references\n3. find_referencing_symbols: Trace dependencies\n\n**Verification Phase**:\n1. Task (quality-engineer): Spawn verification agent (STRICT only)\n2. Bash: Run tests directly (STANDARD)\n3. think_about_task_adherence: Reflect on completeness\n\n**Completion Phase**:\n1. write_memory: Save session state\n2. think_about_whether_you_are_done: Final check\n\n## Examples\n\n### STRICT Task\n```\n/sc:task \"implement user authentication with JWT\"\n\n-> Classified as STRICT (security domain, authentication keyword)\n-> Full 6-category checklist enforced\n-> Verification agent spawned\n-> Adversarial questions answered\n```\n\n### STANDARD Task\n```\n/sc:task \"add pagination to user list\"\n\n-> Classified as STANDARD (add keyword, typical feature)\n-> Context loaded before editing\n-> Downstream impacts checked\n-> Direct test execution\n```\n\n### LIGHT Task\n```\n/sc:task \"fix typo in README\"\n\n-> Classified as LIGHT (trivial keyword, documentation path)\n-> Quick sanity check only\n-> No verification delay\n```\n\n### EXEMPT Task\n```\n/sc:task \"explain how the auth flow works\"\n\n-> Classified as EXEMPT (explain pattern detected)\n-> Immediate execution\n-> No compliance overhead\n```\n\n### Override Example\n```\n/sc:task \"update config file\" --compliance strict\n\n-> User override to STRICT regardless of classification\n-> Full verification enforced\n```\n\n## Boundaries\n\n**Will:**\n- Classify tasks into appropriate compliance tiers\n- Enforce tier-appropriate verification requirements\n- Provide confidence scoring with rationale\n- Track feedback for continuous calibration\n- Support user overrides with justification\n\n**Will Not:**\n- Skip safety-critical verification for STRICT tasks\n- Apply STRICT overhead to genuinely trivial changes\n- Override user's explicit compliance choice\n- Proceed with <70% confidence without user confirmation\n\n## Success Criteria\n\n| Metric | Target | Measurement |\n|--------|--------|-------------|\n| Tier classification accuracy | >=80% | User feedback on appropriateness |\n| User confusion rate | <10% | \"Which command?\" questions eliminated |\n| Skip rate (--skip-compliance) | <12% | Override tracking |\n| Regression prevention | >=85% | Post-verification bug detection |\n| STRICT tier overhead | <25% | Execution telemetry |\n\n## Configuration References\n\n- Keywords: `config/tier-keywords.yaml`\n- Verification routing: `config/verification-routing.yaml`\n- Acceptance criteria: `config/tier-acceptance-criteria.yaml`\n- Circuit breakers: See MCP.md\n- Routing logic: See ORCHESTRATOR.md\n\n\nARGUMENTS: Execute all tasks in Define per-phase contract schemas (`portify-prerequisites.yaml`, `portify-analysis.yaml`, `portify-spec.yaml`, `portify-codegen.yaml`, `portify-integration.yaml`, return contract)\n   124→4. Implement contract validation logic — next phase reads and validates incoming contract before proceeding\n   125→5. Implement return contract structure (FR-043): status, failure_phase, failure_type, generated_files, counts, api_snapshot_hash, resume_command, warnings, phase_contracts\n   126→6. Emit `portify-prerequisites.yaml` contract (FR-014)\n   148→\n   149→**Requirements**: FR-010 through FR-014, FR-047, NFR-013, NFR-014\n   150→**Risks mitigated**: RISK-001, RISK-003, RISK-008, RISK-012\n   151→\n   152→### Milestone 2.3: Phase 1 — Workflow Analysis\n   153→\n   154→1. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_DP0dPyFfSCdT5wZwNnfJXSg4","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→---\n     2→name: sc-cli-portify\n     3→description: \"Port inference-based SuperClaude workflows (commands + skills + agents) into programmatic CLI pipelines with sprint-style supervised execution. Use this skill when you need to convert any multi-step workflow into a reliable, repeatable CLI runner that uses Python to control flow and Claude to fill in content. Triggers on: portify, CLI pipeline, programmatic workflow, convert skill to CLI, workflow runner, deterministic pipeline, move to code, make repeatable, ensure consistency.\"\n     4→allowed-tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, Bash, TodoWrite, Task\n     5→---\n     6→\n     7→> **DEPRECATED**: This skill has been replaced by `sc-cli-portify-protocol/`.\n     8→> The protocol skill contains the full behavioral protocol with updated API-aligned refs.\n     9→> This directory is scheduled for removal after Phase 5 validation.\n    10→> Do NOT use this skill for new work — use `Skill sc:cli-portify-protocol` instead.\n    11→\n    12→# sc:cli-portify — Workflow-to-CLI Pipeline Compiler\n    13→\n    14→Convert inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines that ensure 100% completion and consistency between runs.\n    15→\n    16→## Purpose\n    17→\n    18→SuperClaude workflows implemented as commands + skills + agents + refs rely entirely on Claude's reasoning for orchestration. This works well for flexible, one-off tasks but breaks down for repeatable multi-step pipelines where you need:\n    19→\n    20→- **Deterministic control flow** — Python decides what runs when, not inference\n    21→- **Formal artifact validation** — Gates check output quality programmatically\n    22→- **Resume/retry** — Failed steps resume without re-running everything\n    23→- **Live monitoring** — TUI shows progress, stall detection, diagnostics\n    24→- **Budget economics** — Turn ledger tracks consumption across subprocesses\n    25→- **Consistent behavior** — Same inputs produce structurally identical outputs every run\n    26→\n    27→The existing `pipeline/` and `sprint/` modules already solve these problems. This skill bridges the gap by analyzing a workflow and generating a new CLI module that uses these proven patterns.\n    28→\n    29→## What Gets Generated\n    30→\n    31→A CLI subcommand package under `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`:\n    32→\n    33→| File | Purpose | When Generated |\n    34→|------|---------|----------------|\n    35→| `models.py` | Domain types extending `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult` | Always |\n    36→| `gates.py` | `GateCriteria` + semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]` | Always |\n    37→| `prompts.py` | LLM contract builders per Claude-assisted step | Always |\n    38→| `config.py` | CLI arg resolution, file discovery, config construction | Always |\n    39→| `executor.py` | Synchronous supervisor loop with `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism | Always |\n    40→| `monitor.py` | NDJSON incremental output parser with domain signals | Always |\n    41→| `process.py` | `ClaudeProcess` subclass with domain-specific prompt building | Always |\n    42→| `tui.py` | Rich live dashboard with gate state machine | Always |\n    43→| `logging_.py` | Dual JSONL + Markdown execution logging | Always |\n    44→| `diagnostics.py` | `DiagnosticCollector`, `FailureClassifier`, `ReportGenerator` | Always |\n    45→| `commands.py` | Click CLI group and subcommands | Always |\n    46→| `__init__.py` | Package exports | Always |\n    47→| `inventory.py` | Pure-programmatic file discovery/classification | If workflow has discovery steps |\n    48→| `filtering.py` | Pure-programmatic inter-step data filtering | If workflow has filtering between steps |\n    49→\n    50→Plus integration patches for `main.py`.\n    51→\n    52→## Required Input\n    53→\n    54→```\n    55→/sc:cli-portify --workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>]\n    56→```\n    57→\n    58→| Argument | Required | Description |\n    59→|----------|----------|-------------|\n    60→| `--workflow` | Yes | Skill directory path or `sc-*` name to portify |\n    61→| `--name` | No | CLI subcommand name (default: derived from workflow) |\n    62→| `--output` | No | Output directory (default: `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`) |\n    63→| `--dry-run` | No | Show decomposition plan without generating code |\n    64→| `--skip-integration` | No | Generate module only, skip main.py wiring |\n    65→\n    66→**STOP** if `--workflow` is not provided or the path doesn't resolve to a valid skill.\n    67→\n    68→## Behavioral Flow\n    69→\n    70→Four phases, each producing artifacts that feed the next. Present output to user for review after Phases 1 and 2 before continuing.\n    71→\n    72→### Phase 1: Workflow Analysis\n    73→\n    74→Load `refs/analysis-protocol.md` before this phase. It contains the discovery checklist, step decomposition algorithm, classification rubric, and output format template.\n    75→\n    76→**Goal**: Decompose the target workflow into a structured pipeline specification.\n    77→\n    78→1. **Discover components** — Find the command `.md`, skill `SKILL.md`, all `refs/` and `rules/`, all `templates/`, all `scripts/`, and all referenced agents. Build a component inventory table with paths, line counts, and purposes.\n    79→\n    80→2. **Map the protocol** — Extract the step-by-step behavioral flow. Pay attention to:\n    81→   - Wave/phase boundaries in the skill\n    82→   - Agent delegation patterns (which agent, parallel vs sequential)\n    83→   - Conditional execution paths (flags that skip steps)\n    84→   - Implicit inter-step data flow (what one step produces that another consumes)\n    85→\n    86→3. **Identify steps** — A new Step boundary exists when:\n    87→   - A new artifact is produced\n    88→   - A different agent takes over\n    89→   - Execution mode changes (sequential → parallel)\n    90→   - A quality gate must be evaluated\n    91→   - Operation type changes (analysis → generation → validation)\n    92→\n    93→4. **Classify each step** on the programmatic spectrum:\n    94→   - **Pure programmatic**: Runs as a Python function, no Claude subprocess. Examples: file inventory via `git ls-files`, config construction, file filtering between passes, scoring formulas, structural validation, batch assignment. If the logic is deterministic and the input/output formats are well-defined, it should be programmatic.\n    95→   - **Claude-assisted**: Runs as a Claude subprocess with a prompt contract and gate. Examples: content analysis, writing reports, rubric-based assessment, creative synthesis, debate.\n    96→   - **Hybrid**: Programmatic setup → Claude subprocess → programmatic validation. Examples: steps that need Claude for content but have strict structural validation on the output.\n    97→\n    98→5. **Map dependencies and parallel groups** — Draw the data flow: which steps produce artifacts consumed by downstream steps? Which steps are independent and can run concurrently? For batched workflows, identify where batch parallelism applies.\n    99→\n   100→6. **Extract gates** — For each step output, identify what validation the original workflow expects. Map these to gate tiers: EXEMPT (informational), LIGHT (exists + non-empty), STANDARD (structural checks), STRICT (structural + semantic).\n   101→\n   102→7. **Assign gate modes** — `GateMode.BLOCKING` for steps whose output feeds downstream steps. `GateMode.TRAILING` for quality-only checks that don't affect data flow.\n   103→\n   104→**Output**: `portify-analysis.md` — Complete decomposition following the template in `refs/analysis-protocol.md`. Must include: component inventory, step graph with classifications, parallel groups, gates summary, data flow diagram, and recommendations.\n   105→\n   106→**Present to user for review before Phase 2.**\n   107→\n   108→### Phase 2: Pipeline Specification\n   109→\n   110→Load `refs/pipeline-spec.md` before this phase. It contains Step definition patterns, model design patterns, gate patterns, executor patterns, and the new unified-audit-gating patterns (TurnLedger, gate modes, subprocess isolation, context injection, resume semantics).\n   111→\n   112→**Goal**: Convert the analysis into concrete, code-ready specifications.\n   113→\n   114→1. **Design the Step graph** — Map each workflow step to `Step` objects. Batched parallel steps are `list[Step]` groups. Dynamic step counts (e.g., batch count determined at runtime) require a `build_steps()` function.\n   115→\n   116→2. **Define models** — Design domain-specific dataclasses:\n   117→   - Config extending `PipelineConfig` with workflow-specific fields (source paths, pass selection, batch sizes, stall/timeout policies)\n   118→   - Status enum with domain-specific states (e.g., `VALIDATION_FAIL`, `CRITICAL_FAIL` for audit workflows)\n   119→   - Result extending `StepResult` with execution telemetry\n   120→   - Aggregate result with `resume_command()` and `suggested_resume_budget`\n   121→   - Monitor state with domain-specific NDJSON signals\n   122→   - `TurnLedger` integration for budget tracking\n   123→\n   124→3. **Design prompts** — For each Claude-assisted step, write the prompt builder. Each prompt must specify:\n   125→   - What input files/context to embed (inline for <50KB, `--file` args for larger)\n   126→   - Required output sections and frontmatter fields\n   127→   - Machine-readable markers: `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE|HALT`\n   128→   - Any structural requirements the gate will check\n   129→\n   130→   If prompts collectively exceed ~300 lines, put them in a separate `portify-prompts.md`.\n   131→\n   132→4. **Design gates** — For each output artifact, define:\n   133→   - `GateCriteria` with tier, required frontmatter, min lines, semantic checks\n   134→   - `GateMode` (BLOCKING or TRAILING)\n   135→   - All semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]`\n   136→\n   137→5. **Implement pure-programmatic steps** — Write actual runnable Python code for steps classified as \"pure programmatic\". These run as direct function calls in the executor, bypassing `ClaudeProcess`. Include: file discovery, domain classification, batch assignment, inter-pass filtering, config construction.\n   138→\n   139→6. **Design the executor** — Sprint-style synchronous supervisor:\n   140→   - `ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism (NOT async/await)\n   141→   - `time.sleep()` polling loops for monitoring\n   142→   - Per-step/batch monitoring with stall detection\n   143→   - `TurnLedger` budget guards before each launch\n   144→   - Runner-authored truth: reports from observed data, not Claude self-reporting\n   145→   - Subprocess isolation (4-layer model) for child Claude sessions\n   146→   - Context injection: prior results compressed into subsequent prompts\n   147→   - Signal-aware shutdown with `SignalHandler`\n   148→\n   149→7. **Plan integration** — Click command group, main.py import, file generation order.\n   150→\n   151→**Output**: `portify-spec.md` (+ optional `portify-prompts.md`) — Complete code spec with models, gates, executor design, pure-programmatic implementations, and integration plan.\n   152→\n   153→**Present to user for approval before Phase 3.**\n   154→\n   155→### Phase 3: Code Generation\n   156→\n   157→Load `refs/code-templates.md` before this phase. It contains parameterized file templates for every module.\n   158→\n   159→Generate files in dependency order (models → gates → prompts → config → monitor → process → executor → tui → logging → diagnostics → commands → __init__). Each file imports from `superclaude.cli.pipeline` for shared base types and follows sprint/roadmap naming conventions.\n   160→\n   161→### Phase 4: Integration\n   162→\n   163→1. Patch `main.py` — Add import and `app.add_command()`\n   164→2. Verify imports — Quick check for circular dependencies\n   165→3. Generate structural test — Validates step graph, gate definitions, and model consistency\n   166→4. Write `portify-summary.md` — File inventory, CLI usage, step graph, known limitations\n   167→\n   168→## Decision Framework: What Becomes Programmatic\n   169→\n   170→The value of portification is moving orchestration out of inference. Here are concrete examples from tested workflows:\n   171→\n   172→| Operation | Classification | Why |\n   173→|-----------|---------------|-----|\n   174→| Enumerate repo files | Pure programmatic | `git ls-files` + pattern matching is deterministic |\n   175→| Classify file domains | Pure programmatic | Extension/path pattern rules, no judgment needed |\n   176→| Assign files to batches | Pure programmatic | Math: divide files by batch_size, assign to agents |\n   177→| Filter Pass 1 results for Pass 2 | Pure programmatic | Parse markdown, extract classifications, filter list |\n   178→| Analyze file for issues | Claude-assisted | Requires reading code, understanding context |\n   179→| Score variant quality | Hybrid | Formulas are programmatic, rubric evaluation is Claude |\n   180→| Validate structural integrity | Pure programmatic | Regex checks, section presence, field counts |\n   181→| Spot-check verification | Claude-assisted | Re-reads files, re-evaluates claims independently |\n   182→| Merge batch reports | Claude-assisted | Requires synthesis, deduplication, pattern extraction |\n   183→| Compute convergence score | Pure programmatic | `agreed / total` — arithmetic |\n   184→| Generate executive summary | Claude-assisted | Requires narrative synthesis |\n   185→\n   186→### Rule of Thumb\n   187→If you can write a Python function for it with clear input→output types and no ambiguity, make it programmatic. If it requires reading natural language, making judgments, or synthesizing information, use Claude with a gate to verify the output.\n   188→\n   189→## Code Generation Principles\n   190→\n   191→These align with the unified-audit-gating architecture in `pipeline/`.\n   192→\n   193→### Critical Constraints\n   194→1. **Synchronous execution** — Sprint uses `threading` + `time.sleep()` polling. Use `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism. Do NOT use async/await.\n   195→2. **Gate function signatures** — All semantic checks return `tuple[bool, str]`. The reason string feeds diagnostics and retry decisions.\n   196→3. **Runner-authored truth** — Reports derive from runner-observed data (exit codes, artifacts, gates, monitor signals), not Claude's self-reported status.\n   197→4. **Deterministic flow control** — Python makes all decisions about what runs next. Claude never decides \"what's next\".\n   198→\n   199→### Shared Pipeline Patterns\n   200→5. **Reuse `pipeline/` primitives** — `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult`, `GateMode`, `GateCriteria`, `SemanticCheck`, `gate_passed()`, `ClaudeProcess`\n   201→6. **Gate modes** — `BLOCKING` (default) stops pipeline on failure. `TRAILING` defers evaluation. Use trailing only for quality checks that don't affect downstream data flow.\n   202→7. **Turn budget** — `TurnLedger` with debit/credit/guard for multi-subprocess pipelines. Pre-launch guards prevent launching when budget too low.\n   203→8. **Subprocess isolation** — 4-layer model: scoped work dir, git ceiling, isolated plugin dir, isolated settings dir.\n   204→9. **Context injection** — Prior results serialized into subsequent prompts. Compress older, preserve recent.\n   205→10. **Resume-first failures** — On halt: exact resume command, remaining work, diagnostic path, budget suggestion.\n   206→11. **Diagnostic chain** — Runner-side failure analysis that doesn't consume turn budget.\n   207→\n   208→### Output Guidance\n   209→- `portify-analysis.md`: Under 400 lines. Step graph, gates, data flow.\n   210→- `portify-spec.md`: Split prompts to separate file if spec exceeds 800 lines.\n   211→- Pure-programmatic steps: Full implementation code, not descriptions.\n   212→\n   213→## Boundaries\n   214→\n   215→### Will Do\n   216→- Analyze any SuperClaude skill/command/agent workflow\n   217→- Decompose into structured pipeline specification\n   218→- Generate complete CLI subcommand module\n   219→- Wire module into CLI infrastructure\n   220→- Generate structural tests for pipeline\n   221→\n   222→### Will Not Do\n   223→- Execute the generated pipeline\n   224→- Modify original skill/command/agent files\n   225→- Create new skills or agents\n   226→- Make architectural decisions about the workflow's logic\n   227→- Generate LLM content quality tests (only structural)\n   228→\n   229→## Related Commands\n   230→\n   231→- `/sc:implement` — General feature implementation\n   232→- `/sc:design` — System architecture design\n   233→- `/sc:analyze` — Code analysis for understanding workflows\n   234→- `/sc:build` — Build and compile after generating\n   235→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_013s7Jq6Ktd2k1fHEbqKDfny","session_id":"2a0dcd71-e2d6-492b-be01-9ec2d4de516f","uuid":"6deea571-08b5-476e-9551-b6e3fb1ff038"}

Validate claims (Claude, sampling with standard gate)\n   196→\n   197→### Pattern: Extract-Transform-Validate\n   198→Found in: roadmap, tasklist\n   199→\n   200→Steps:\n   201→1. Analyze current state (Claude, standard gate)\n   212→2. Generate plan (Claude, standard gate)\n   213→3. Execute changes (Claude, strict gate)\n   214→4. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_vwEqAHYJvSg9yPE1Gsgl0Tgb","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→---\n     2→name: sc-cli-portify-protocol\n     3→description: \"Full behavioral protocol for sc:cli-portify — port inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines with sprint-style supervised execution.\"\n     4→category: development\n     5→complexity: high\n     6→allowed-tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, Bash, TodoWrite, Task\n     7→mcp-servers: [sequential, serena, context7, auggie-mcp]\n     8→personas: [architect, analyzer, backend]\n     9→argument-hint: \"--workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>] [--dry-run] [--skip-integration]\"\n    10→---\n    11→\n    12→# sc:cli-portify — Workflow-to-CLI Pipeline Compiler\n    13→\n    14→Convert inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines that ensure 100% completion and consistency between runs.\n    15→\n    16→## Purpose\n    17→\n    18→SuperClaude workflows implemented as commands + skills + agents + refs rely entirely on Claude's reasoning for orchestration. This works well for flexible, one-off tasks but breaks down for repeatable multi-step pipelines where you need:\n    19→\n    20→- **Deterministic control flow** — Python decides what runs when, not inference\n    21→- **Formal artifact validation** — Gates check output quality programmatically\n    22→- **Resume/retry** — Failed steps resume without re-running everything\n    23→- **Live monitoring** — TUI shows progress, stall detection, diagnostics\n    24→- **Budget economics** — Turn ledger tracks consumption across subprocesses\n    25→- **Consistent behavior** — Same inputs produce structurally identical outputs every run\n    26→\n    27→The existing `pipeline/` and `sprint/` modules already solve these problems. This skill bridges the gap by analyzing a workflow and generating a new CLI module that uses these proven patterns.\n    28→\n    29→## What Gets Generated\n    30→\n    31→A CLI subcommand package under `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`:\n    32→\n    33→| File | Purpose | When Generated |\n    34→|------|---------|----------------|\n    35→| `models.py` | Domain types extending `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult` | Always |\n    36→| `gates.py` | `GateCriteria` + semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]` | Always |\n    37→| `prompts.py` | LLM contract builders per Claude-assisted step | Always |\n    38→| `config.py` | CLI arg resolution, file discovery, config construction | Always |\n    39→| `executor.py` | Synchronous supervisor loop with `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism | Always |\n    40→| `monitor.py` | NDJSON incremental output parser with domain signals | Always |\n    41→| `process.py` | `ClaudeProcess` subclass with domain-specific prompt building | Always |\n    42→| `tui.py` | Rich live dashboard with gate state machine | Always |\n    43→| `logging_.py` | Dual JSONL + Markdown execution logging | Always |\n    44→| `diagnostics.py` | `DiagnosticCollector`, `FailureClassifier`, `ReportGenerator` | Always |\n    45→| `commands.py` | Click CLI group and subcommands | Always |\n    46→| `__init__.py` | Package exports | Always |\n    47→| `inventory.py` | Pure-programmatic file discovery/classification | If workflow has discovery steps |\n    48→| `filtering.py` | Pure-programmatic inter-step data filtering | If workflow has filtering between steps |\n    49→\n    50→Plus integration patches for `main.py`.\n    51→\n    52→## Required Input\n    53→\n    54→```\n    55→/sc:cli-portify --workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>]\n    56→```\n    57→\n    58→| Argument | Required | Description |\n    59→|----------|----------|-------------|\n    60→| `--workflow` | Yes | Skill directory path or `sc-*` name to portify |\n    61→| `--name` | No | CLI subcommand name (default: derived from workflow) |\n    62→| `--output` | No | Output directory (default: `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`) |\n    63→| `--dry-run` | No | Show decomposition plan without generating code |\n    64→| `--skip-integration` | No | Generate module only, skip main.py wiring |\n    65→\n    66→**STOP** if `--workflow` is not provided or the path doesn't resolve to a valid skill.\n    67→\n    68→## Behavioral Flow\n    69→\n    70→Four phases, each producing artifacts that feed the next. Present output to user for review after Phases 1 and 2 before continuing.\n    71→\n    72→### Phase 1: Workflow Analysis\n    73→\n    74→Load `refs/analysis-protocol.md` before this phase. It contains the discovery checklist, step decomposition algorithm, classification rubric, and output format template.\n    75→\n    76→**Goal**: Decompose the target workflow into a structured pipeline specification.\n    77→\n    78→1. **Discover components** — Find the command `.md`, skill `SKILL.md`, all `refs/` and `rules/`, all `templates/`, all `scripts/`, and all referenced agents. Build a component inventory table with paths, line counts, and purposes.\n    79→\n    80→2. **Map the protocol** — Extract the step-by-step behavioral flow. Pay attention to:\n    81→   - Wave/phase boundaries in the skill\n    82→   - Agent delegation patterns (which agent, parallel vs sequential)\n    83→   - Conditional execution paths (flags that skip steps)\n    84→   - Implicit inter-step data flow (what one step produces that another consumes)\n    85→\n    86→3. **Identify steps** — A new Step boundary exists when:\n    87→   - A new artifact is produced\n    88→   - A different agent takes over\n    89→   - Execution mode changes (sequential → parallel)\n    90→   - A quality gate must be evaluated\n    91→   - Operation type changes (analysis → generation → validation)\n    92→\n    93→4. **Classify each step** on the programmatic spectrum:\n    94→   - **Pure programmatic**: Runs as a Python function, no Claude subprocess. Examples: file inventory via `git ls-files`, config construction, file filtering between passes, scoring formulas, structural validation, batch assignment. If the logic is deterministic and the input/output formats are well-defined, it should be programmatic.\n    95→   - **Claude-assisted**: Runs as a Claude subprocess with a prompt contract and gate. Examples: content analysis, writing reports, rubric-based assessment, creative synthesis, debate.\n    96→   - **Hybrid**: Programmatic setup → Claude subprocess → programmatic validation. Examples: steps that need Claude for content but have strict structural validation on the output.\n    97→\n    98→5. **Map dependencies and parallel groups** — Draw the data flow: which steps produce artifacts consumed by downstream steps? Which steps are independent and can run concurrently? For batched workflows, identify where batch parallelism applies.\n    99→\n   100→6. **Extract gates** — For each step output, identify what validation the original workflow expects. Map these to gate tiers: EXEMPT (informational), LIGHT (exists + non-empty), STANDARD (structural checks), STRICT (structural + semantic).\n   101→\n   102→7. **Assign gate modes** — `GateMode.BLOCKING` for steps whose output feeds downstream steps. `GateMode.TRAILING` for quality-only checks that don't affect data flow.\n   103→\n   104→**Output**: `portify-analysis.md` — Complete decomposition following the template in `refs/analysis-protocol.md`. Must include: component inventory, step graph with classifications, parallel groups, gates summary, data flow diagram, and recommendations.\n   105→\n   106→**Present to user for review before Phase 2.**\n   107→\n   108→### Phase 2: Pipeline Specification\n   109→\n   110→Load `refs/pipeline-spec.md` before this phase. It contains Step definition patterns, model design patterns, gate patterns, executor patterns, and the new unified-audit-gating patterns (TurnLedger, gate modes, subprocess isolation, context injection, resume semantics).\n   111→\n   112→**Goal**: Convert the analysis into concrete, code-ready specifications.\n   113→\n   114→1. **Design the Step graph** — Map each workflow step to `Step` objects. Batched parallel steps are `list[Step]` groups. Dynamic step counts (e.g., batch count determined at runtime) require a `build_steps()` function.\n   115→\n   116→2. **Define models** — Design domain-specific dataclasses:\n   117→   - Config extending `PipelineConfig` with workflow-specific fields (source paths, pass selection, batch sizes, stall/timeout policies)\n   118→   - Status enum with domain-specific states (e.g., `VALIDATION_FAIL`, `CRITICAL_FAIL` for audit workflows)\n   119→   - Result extending `StepResult` with execution telemetry\n   120→   - Aggregate result with `resume_command()` and `suggested_resume_budget`\n   121→   - Monitor state with domain-specific NDJSON signals\n   122→   - `TurnLedger` integration for budget tracking\n   123→\n   124→3. **Design prompts** — For each Claude-assisted step, write the prompt builder. Each prompt must specify:\n   125→   - What input files/context to embed (inline for <50KB, `--file` args for larger)\n   126→   - Required output sections and frontmatter fields\n   127→   - Machine-readable markers: `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE|HALT`\n   128→   - Any structural requirements the gate will check\n   129→\n   130→   If prompts collectively exceed ~300 lines, put them in a separate `portify-prompts.md`.\n   131→\n   132→4. **Design gates** — For each output artifact, define:\n   133→   - `GateCriteria` with tier, required frontmatter, min lines, semantic checks\n   134→   - `GateMode` (BLOCKING or TRAILING)\n   135→   - All semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]`\n   136→\n   137→5. **Implement pure-programmatic steps** — Write actual runnable Python code for steps classified as \"pure programmatic\". These run as direct function calls in the executor, bypassing `ClaudeProcess`. Include: file discovery, domain classification, batch assignment, inter-pass filtering, config construction.\n   138→\n   139→6. **Design the executor** — Sprint-style synchronous supervisor:\n   140→   - `ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism (NOT async/await)\n   141→   - `time.sleep()` polling loops for monitoring\n   142→   - Per-step/batch monitoring with stall detection\n   143→   - `TurnLedger` budget guards before each launch\n   144→   - Runner-authored truth: reports from observed data, not Claude self-reporting\n   145→   - Subprocess isolation (4-layer model) for child Claude sessions\n   146→   - Context injection: prior results compressed into subsequent prompts\n   147→   - Signal-aware shutdown with `SignalHandler`\n   148→\n   149→7. **Plan integration** — Click command group, main.py import, file generation order.\n   150→\n   151→**Output**: `portify-spec.md` (+ optional `portify-prompts.md`) — Complete code spec with models, gates, executor design, pure-programmatic implementations, and integration plan.\n   152→\n   153→**Present to user for approval before Phase 3.**\n   154→\n   155→### Phase 3: Code Generation\n   156→\n   157→Load `refs/code-templates.md` before this phase. It contains parameterized file templates for every module.\n   158→\n   159→Generate files in dependency order (models → gates → prompts → config → monitor → process → executor → tui → logging → diagnostics → commands → __init__). Each file imports from `superclaude.cli.pipeline` for shared base types and follows sprint/roadmap naming conventions.\n   160→\n   161→### Phase 4: Integration\n   162→\n   163→1. Patch `main.py` — Add import and `app.add_command()`\n   164→2. Verify imports — Quick check for circular dependencies\n   165→3. Generate structural test — Validates step graph, gate definitions, and model consistency\n   166→4. Write `portify-summary.md` — File inventory, CLI usage, step graph, known limitations\n   167→\n   168→## Decision Framework: What Becomes Programmatic\n   169→\n   170→The value of portification is moving orchestration out of inference. Here are concrete examples from tested workflows:\n   171→\n   172→| Operation | Classification | Why |\n   173→|-----------|---------------|-----|\n   174→| Enumerate repo files | Pure programmatic | `git ls-files` + pattern matching is deterministic |\n   175→| Classify file domains | Pure programmatic | Extension/path pattern rules, no judgment needed |\n   176→| Assign files to batches | Pure programmatic | Math: divide files by batch_size, assign to agents |\n   177→| Filter Pass 1 results for Pass 2 | Pure programmatic | Parse markdown, extract classifications, filter list |\n   178→| Analyze file for issues | Claude-assisted | Requires reading code, understanding context |\n   179→| Score variant quality | Hybrid | Formulas are programmatic, rubric evaluation is Claude |\n   180→| Validate structural integrity | Pure programmatic | Regex checks, section presence, field counts |\n   181→| Spot-check verification | Claude-assisted | Re-reads files, re-evaluates claims independently |\n   182→| Merge batch reports | Claude-assisted | Requires synthesis, deduplication, pattern extraction |\n   183→| Compute convergence score | Pure programmatic | `agreed / total` — arithmetic |\n   184→| Generate executive summary | Claude-assisted | Requires narrative synthesis |\n   185→\n   186→### Rule of Thumb\n   187→If you can write a Python function for it with clear input→output types and no ambiguity, make it programmatic. If it requires reading natural language, making judgments, or synthesizing information, use Claude with a gate to verify the output.\n   188→\n   189→## Code Generation Principles\n   190→\n   191→These align with the unified-audit-gating architecture in `pipeline/`.\n   192→\n   193→### Critical Constraints\n   194→1. **Synchronous execution** — Sprint uses `threading` + `time.sleep()` polling. Use `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism. Do NOT use async/await.\n   195→2. **Gate function signatures** — All semantic checks return `tuple[bool, str]`. The reason string feeds diagnostics and retry decisions.\n   196→3. **Runner-authored truth** — Reports derive from runner-observed data (exit codes, artifacts, gates, monitor signals), not Claude's self-reported status.\n   197→4. **Deterministic flow control** — Python makes all decisions about what runs next. Claude never decides \"what's next\".\n   198→\n   199→### Shared Pipeline Patterns\n   200→5. **Reuse `pipeline/` primitives** — `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult`, `GateMode`, `GateCriteria`, `SemanticCheck`, `gate_passed()`, `ClaudeProcess`\n   201→6. **Gate modes** — `BLOCKING` (default) stops pipeline on failure. `TRAILING` defers evaluation. Use trailing only for quality checks that don't affect downstream data flow.\n   202→7. **Turn budget** — `TurnLedger` with debit/credit/guard for multi-subprocess pipelines. Pre-launch guards prevent launching when budget too low.\n   203→8. **Subprocess isolation** — 4-layer model: scoped work dir, git ceiling, isolated plugin dir, isolated settings dir.\n   204→9. **Context injection** — Prior results serialized into subsequent prompts. Compress older, preserve recent.\n   205→10. **Resume-first failures** — On halt: exact resume command, remaining work, diagnostic path, budget suggestion.\n   206→11. **Diagnostic chain** — Runner-side failure analysis that doesn't consume turn budget.\n   207→\n   208→### Output Guidance\n   209→- `portify-analysis.md`: Under 400 lines. Step graph, gates, data flow.\n   210→- `portify-spec.md`: Split prompts to separate file if spec exceeds 800 lines.\n   211→- Pure-programmatic steps: Full implementation code, not descriptions.\n   212→\n   213→## Boundaries\n   214→\n   215→### Will Do\n   216→- Analyze any SuperClaude skill/command/agent workflow\n   217→- Decompose into structured pipeline specification\n   218→- Generate complete CLI subcommand module\n   219→- Wire module into CLI infrastructure\n   220→- Generate structural tests for pipeline\n   221→\n   222→### Will Not Do\n   223→- Execute the generated pipeline\n   224→- Modify original skill/command/agent files\n   225→- Create new skills or agents\n   226→- Make architectural decisions about the workflow's logic\n   227→- Generate LLM content quality tests (only structural)\n   228→\n   229→## Related Commands\n   230→\n   231→- `/sc:implement` — General feature implementation\n   232→- `/sc:design` — System architecture design\n   233→- `/sc:analyze` — Code analysis for understanding workflows\n   234→- `/sc:build` — Build and compile after generating\n   235→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_013s7Jq6Ktd2k1fHEbqKDfny","session_id":"2a0dcd71-e2d6-492b-be01-9ec2d4de516f","uuid":"6c08b5dc-53ce-4f2c-a654-a244ecd87bfb"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_ccbChi7OoirqaOTiGE3v1CGX","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Workflow Analysis Protocol\n     2→\n     3→Loaded during Phase 1 of portification. Guides the systematic decomposition of an inference-based workflow into a pipeline specification.\n     4→\n     5→## Discovery Checklist\n     6→\n     7→When analyzing a target workflow, locate and read these components in order:\n     8→\n     9→### 1. Find the Command\n    10→\n    11→Look in `src/superclaude/commands/` for the matching command file. Extract:\n    12→- Frontmatter: category, complexity, mcp-servers, personas\n    13→- Behavioral Flow section: the high-level step sequence\n    14→- Arguments and options\n    15→- Boundaries (Will Do / Will Not Do)\n    16→\n    17→### 2. Find the Skill\n    18→\n    19→Look in `src/superclaude/skills/` for the matching skill directory. Read:\n    20→- `SKILL.md`: The full protocol with step-by-step behavioral flow\n    21→- `refs/`: All reference files (algorithms, templates, scoring protocols)\n    22→- `rules/`: Validation rules and classification taxonomies\n    23→- `templates/`: Output format templates (these become gate validation targets)\n    24→- `scripts/`: Shell scripts for preprocessing (these become pure-programmatic steps)\n    25→- Note which refs are loaded at which phase/wave\n    26→\n    27→### 3. Find the Agents\n    28→\n    29→Search for agents referenced in the skill:\n    30→- Look in `src/superclaude/agents/` for `.md` files\n    31→- Note each agent's: triggers, tools, responsibilities, boundaries\n    32→- Identify delegation patterns: parallel vs sequential, orchestrator vs worker\n    33→\n    34→### 4. Map the Data Flow\n    35→\n    36→Trace how data moves through the workflow:\n    37→- What inputs does each step consume?\n    38→- What artifacts does each step produce?\n    39→- Which artifacts feed into downstream steps?\n    40→- What is the final output?\n    41→\n    42→## Step Decomposition Algorithm\n    43→\n    44→For each discrete operation in the workflow:\n    45→\n    46→### Identify Step Boundaries\n    47→\n    48→A new Step starts when:\n    49→- A new artifact is produced\n    50→- A different agent takes over\n    51→- The execution mode changes (sequential → parallel)\n    52→- A quality gate must be evaluated\n    53→- The operation type changes (analysis → generation → validation)\n    54→\n    55→### Classify Each Step\n    56→\n    57→Rate each step on the **programmatic spectrum**:\n    58→\n    59→| Classification | Characteristics | Implementation |\n    60→|---------------|-----------------|----------------|\n    61→| **Pure Programmatic** | Deterministic, formula-based, structural | Python function, no Claude |\n    62→| **Programmatic + Validation** | Structural check with semantic judgment | Python primary, Claude for edge cases |\n    63→| **Claude-Assisted** | Content generation within structured format | Claude subprocess with gate validation |\n    64→| **Pure Inference** | Creative, subjective, context-dependent | Claude subprocess with light gate |\n    65→\n    66→### Map to Pipeline Primitives\n    67→\n    68→| Workflow Concept | Pipeline Primitive |\n    69→|-----------------|-------------------|\n    70→| Sequential step | `Step` in step list |\n    71→| Parallel operations | `list[Step]` parallel group |\n    72→| Quality gate | `GateCriteria` on the Step |\n    73→| Agent delegation | Prompt in `prompts.py` |\n    74→| Scoring formula | Python function in `gates.py` or separate module |\n    75→| Status decision | `_determine_status()` in `executor.py` |\n    76→| Input validation | `config.py` validation logic |\n    77→| Output format | Frontmatter requirements in gate + prompt |\n    78→| Retry on failure | `retry_limit` on Step |\n    79→| Skip on success | Resume logic in executor |\n    80→\n    81→## Analysis Output Format\n    82→\n    83→Phase 1 produces `portify-analysis.md` with this structure:\n    84→\n    85→```markdown\n    86→---\n    87→source_skill: <skill-name>\n    88→source_command: <command-name>\n    89→step_count: <N>\n    90→parallel_groups: <N>\n    91→gate_count: <N>\n    92→agent_count: <N>\n    93→complexity: simple|moderate|high\n 106→\n   107→## Step Graph\n   108→\n   109→### Step 1: {name}\n   110→- **Type**: pure-programmatic | claude-assisted | hybrid\n   111→- **Inputs**: [list of input artifacts]\n   112→- **Output**: artifact-name.md\n   113→- **Gate**: exempt | light | standard | strict\n   114→- **Agent**: none | agent-name\n   115→- **Parallel**: yes (with step N) | no\n   116→- **Timeout**: Ns\n   117→- **Retry**: N attempts\n   118→- **Notes**: any special considerations\n   119→\n   120→### Step 2: {name}\n   121→...\n   122→\n   123→## Parallel Groups\n   124→\n   125→| Group | Steps | Rationale |\n   126→|-------|-------|-----------|\n   127→| 1 | step-2, step-3 | Independent variant generation |\n   128→\n   129→## Gates Summary\n   130→\n   131→| Step | Tier | Frontmatter | Min Lines | Semantic Checks |\n   132→|------|------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|\n   133→| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |\n   134→\n   135→## Agent Delegation Map\n   136→\n   137→| Agent | Used In Steps | Parallel | Contract |\n   138→|-------|--------------|----------|----------|\n   139→| ... | ... | ... | ... |\n   140→\n   141→## Data Flow Diagram\n   142→\n   143→```\n   144→[input] → step-1 → [artifact-1]\n   145→                         ↓\n   146→                    ┌─step-2─┐\n   147→                    │         │ (parallel)\n   148→                    └─step-3─┘\n   149→                         ↓\n   150→               [artifact-2, artifact-3]\n   151→                         ↓\n   152→                    step-4 → [final-output]\n   153→```\n   154→\n   155→## Classification Summary\n   156→\n   157→| Category | Count | Steps |\n   158→|----------|-------|-------|\n   159→| Pure Programmatic | N | ... |\n   160→| Claude-Assisted | N | ... |\n   161→| Hybrid | N | ... |\n   162→| Pure Inference | N | ... |\n   163→\n   164→## Recommendations\n   165→\n   166→- Things that should definitely be programmatic but are currently inference\n   167→- Steps that could be split for better gate coverage\n   168→- Opportunities for parallelization\n   169→- Potential failure modes and how to handle them\n   170→```\n   171→\n   172→## Common Workflow Patterns\n   173→\n   174→### Pattern: Multi-Agent Debate\n   175→Found in: adversarial, spec-panel\n   176→\n   177→Steps:\n   178→1. Parse inputs and validate (programmatic)\n   179→2. Generate variants in parallel (Claude, parallel group)\n   180→3. Diff analysis (Claude, single step with strict gate)\n   181→4. Debate rounds (Claude, sequential with convergence gate)\n   182→5. Score and select (hybrid — formulas are programmatic, rubrics are Claude)\n   183→6. Generate plan (Claude, standard gate)\n   184→7. Execute merge (Claude, strict gate with post-validation)\n   185→\n   186→### Pattern: Multi-Pass Audit\n   187→Found in: cleanup-audit\n   188→\n   189→Steps:\n   190→1. Discover files (programmatic)\n   191→2. Surface scan with batching (Claude, batched parallel)\n   192→3. Deep analysis per file (Claude, batched parallel)\n   193→4. Cross-cutting comparison (Claude, single with strict gate)\n   194→5. Consolidate findings (Claude, standard gate)\n   195→6. Validate claims (Claude, sampling with standard gate)\n   196→\n   197→### Pattern: Extract-Transform-Validate\n   198→Found in: roadmap, tasklist\n   199→\n   200→Steps:\n   201→1. Extract requirements (Claude, strict gate on frontmatter)\n   202→2. Generate artifacts (Claude, parallel for multiple agents)\n   203→3. Compare/analyze (Claude, standard gate)\n   204→4. Synthesize final (Claude, strict gate)\n   205→5. Validate structure (programmatic)\n   206→\n   207→### Pattern: Iterative Refinement\n   208→Found in: implement, improve\n   209→\n   210→Steps:\n   211→1. Analyze current state (Claude, standard gate)\n   212→2. Generate plan (Claude, standard gate)\n   213→3. Execute changes (Claude, strict gate)\n   214→4. Validate changes (programmatic + Claude)\n   215→5. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_vsWkKp9Jzih2c7o4mI0xhI6A","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Pipeline Specification Reference\n     2→\n     3→Loaded during Phase 2 of portification. This ref provides the detailed specification patterns for converting a workflow analysis into concrete code designs.\n     4→\n     5→## Step Graph Design\n     6→\n     7→### Step Definition Pattern\n     8→\n     9→Every workflow operation that produces an artifact maps to a `Step`:\n    10→\n    11→```python\n    12→Step(\n    13→    id=\"step-id\",              # kebab-case, unique within pipeline\n    14→    prompt=build_X_prompt(),   # Function from prompts.py\n    15→    output_file=\"artifact.md\", # Relative to work_dir\n    16→    gate=STEP_GATE,            # From gates.py\n    17→    gate_mode=GateMode.BLOCKING,  # BLOCKING (default) or TRAILING\n    18→    timeout_seconds=600,       # Per-step timeout\n    19→    inputs=[\"dep1.md\"],        # Files this step reads\n    20→    retry_limit=1,             # How many retries on gate failure\n    21→    model=None,                # None = use default from config\n    22→)\n    23→```\n    24→\n    25→### Gate Mode Selection\n    26→\n    27→Choose `GateMode` based on step criticality:\n    28→- `BLOCKING` (default): Pipeline halts if gate fails. Use for steps whose output is consumed by downstream steps.\n    29→- `TRAILING`: Gate evaluates asynchronously; pipeline continues. Use for quality checks that don't affect downstream data flow.\n    30→\n    31→Rule of thumb from `resolve_gate_mode()`:\n    32→- Release scope → always BLOCKING\n    33→- Milestone scope → configurable\n    34→- Task scope → TRAILING when `grace_period > 0`\n    35→\n    36→### Parallel Group Pattern\n    37→\n    38→Steps that can run concurrently are wrapped in a list:\n    39→\n    40→```python\n    41→steps = [\n    42→    single_step_1,\n    43→    [parallel_step_a, parallel_step_b],  # Run concurrently\n    44→    single_step_2,                        # Waits for parallel group\n    45→]\n    46→```\n    47→\n    48→The generic `execute_pipeline()` handles thread coordination. If using sprint-style custom executor, implement your own parallel dispatch.\n    49→\n    50→### Dependency Rules\n    51→\n    52→- Steps consume outputs of prior steps via `inputs` list\n    53→- A step can only reference outputs from steps that precede it in the graph\n    54→- Parallel steps within a group must not depend on each other\n    55→- Resume logic skips steps whose output already passes their gate\n    56→\n    57→## Model Design Patterns\n    58→\n    59→### Config Model\n    60→\n    61→Always extend `PipelineConfig`:\n    62→\n    63→```python\n    64→@dataclass\n    65→class MyConfig(PipelineConfig):\n    66→    \"\"\"Pipeline-specific configuration.\"\"\"\n    67→    # Required: identify the input\n    68→    source_path: Path\n    69→\n    70→    # Optional: control behavior\n    71→    depth: str = \"standard\"       # quick|standard|deep\n    72→\n    73→    # Computed paths\n    74→    @property\n    75→    def results_dir(self) -> Path:\n    76→        return self.work_dir / \"results\"\n    77→\n    78→    @property\n    79→    def output_file(self, step_id: str) -> Path:\n    80→        return self.work_dir / f\"{step_id}-output.md\"\n    81→```\n    82→\n    83→### Status Enum\n    84→\n    85→Sprint-style pipelines need richer status than generic `StepStatus`:\n    86→\n    87→```python\n    88→class StepPhaseStatus(Enum):\n    89→    PENDING = \"pending\"\n    90→    RUNNING = \"running\"\n    91→    PASS = \"pass\"\n    92→    PASS_NO_SIGNAL = \"pass_no_signal\"    # Completed but no structured report\n    93→    PASS_NO_REPORT = \"pass_no_report\"    # Output exists but no result file\n    94→    INCOMPLETE = \"incomplete\"             # Budget exhausted\n    95→    HALT = \"halt\"                         # Step recommends stopping\n    96→    TIMEOUT = \"timeout\"                   # Hard timeout exceeded\n    97→    ERROR = \"error\"                       # Process crash\n    98→    SKIPPED = \"skipped\"                   # Skipped by config\n    99→```\n   100→\n   101→### Result Model\n   102→\n   103→Extend `StepResult` with domain telemetry:\n   104→\n   105→```python\n   106→@dataclass\n   107→class MyStepResult(StepResult):\n   108→    exit_code: int | None = None\n   109→    started_at: float | None = None\n   110→    finished_at: float | None = None\n   111→    output_bytes: int = 0\n   112→    error_bytes: int = 0\n   113→    # Domain-specific fields:\n   114→    artifacts_produced: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)\n   115→    gate_details: dict = field(default_factory=dict)\n   116→```\n   117→\n   118→### Monitor State\n   119→\n   120→Domain-specific signals extracted from NDJSON output:\n   121→\n   122→```python\n   123→@dataclass\n   124→class MyMonitorState:\n   125→    output_bytes: int = 0\n   126→    last_growth_time: float = 0.0\n   127→    events_received: int = 0\n   128→    lines_total: int = 0\n   129→    growth_rate_bps: float = 0.0\n   130→    stall_seconds: float = 0.0\n   131→    # Domain-specific signals:\n   132→    last_step_id: str | None = None\n   133→    current_artifact: str | None = None\n   134→    files_changed: set = field(default_factory=set)\n   135→```\n   136→\n   137→## Gate Design Patterns\n   138→\n   139→### Gate Criteria Tiers\n   140→\n   141→```python\n   142→# Exempt: always passes (for optional/informational steps)\n   143→EXEMPT_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   144→    required_frontmatter_fields=[],\n   145→    min_lines=0,\n   146→    enforcement_tier=\"EXEMPT\",\n   147→)\n   148→\n   149→# Light: file exists and non-empty\n   150→LIGHT_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   151→    required_frontmatter_fields=[],\n   152→    min_lines=0,\n   153→    enforcement_tier=\"LIGHT\",\n   154→)\n   155→\n   156→# Standard: structural validation\n   157→STANDARD_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   158→    required_frontmatter_fields=[\"title\", \"status\"],\n   159→    min_lines=50,\n   160→    enforcement_tier=\"STANDARD\",\n   161→)\n   162→\n   163→# Strict: structural + semantic validation\n   164→STRICT_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   165→    required_frontmatter_fields=[\"title\", \"status\", \"convergence_score\"],\n   166→    min_lines=100,\n   167→    enforcement_tier=\"STRICT\",\n   168→    semantic_checks=[\n   169→        SemanticCheck(\n   170→            name=\"no_heading_gaps\",\n   171→            check_fn=_no_heading_gaps,\n   172→            failure_message=\"Heading hierarchy has gaps (e.g. H1→H3 without H2)\",\n   173→        ),\n   174→        SemanticCheck(\n   175→            name=\"refs_resolve\",\n   176→            check_fn=_cross_refs_resolve,\n   177→            failure_message=\"Cross-references do not resolve to valid targets\",\n   178→        ),\n   179→    ],\n   180→)\n   181→```\n   182→\n   183→### Semantic Check Functions\n   184→\n   185→Semantic checks are pure functions matching the `Callable[[str], bool]` signature — they take file content and return a boolean:\n   186→\n   187→```python\n   188→def _no_heading_gaps(content: str) -> bool:\n   189→    \"\"\"Verify heading hierarchy has no gaps (H1->H3 without H2).\"\"\"\n   190→    # ... implementation ...\n   191→    return True  # or False on failure\n   192→\n   193→def _has_required_sections(content: str) -> bool:\n   194→    \"\"\"Verify all required sections are present.\"\"\"\n   195→    required = {\"## Summary\", \"## Findings\", \"## Recommendations\"}\n   196→    present = {line.strip() for line in content.splitlines() if line.startswith(\"## \")}\n   197→    missing = required - present\n   198→    return len(missing) == 0\n   199→```\n   200→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_013s7Jq6Ktd2k1fHEbqKDfny","session_id":"2a0dcd71-e2d6-492b-be01-9ec2d4de516f","uuid":"d6f746eb-7f1c-4f0d-8ad3-f728432eeb1f"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_yC7SXF5lagyMi0fKAv80jtpj","type":"tool_result","content":"   201→### Frontmatter Validation\n   202→\n   203→The shared `gate_passed()` function handles YAML frontmatter checks. Steps that produce markdown with frontmatter get automatic validation:\n   204→\n   205→```yaml\n   206→---\n   207→title: Diff Analysis\n   208→status: complete\n   209→diff_points: 12\n   210→convergence_ready: true\n   211→---\n   212→```\n   213→\n   214→## Prompt Design Patterns\n   215→\n   216→### Prompt Builder Structure\n   217→\n   218→Each step's prompt is a function that takes inputs and returns a complete prompt string:\n   219→\n   220→```python\n   221→def build_analysis_prompt(source_path: Path, config: MyConfig) -> str:\n   222→    return f\"\"\"Read the source file and produce a structured analysis.\n   223→\n   224→Source: {source_path}\n   225→Depth: {config.depth}\n   226→\n   227→## Required Output Format\n   228→\n   229→Write your analysis to the output file with this structure:\n   230→\n   231→```yaml\n   232→---\n   233→title: Analysis Report\n   234→status: complete\n   235→finding_count: <integer>\n   236→---\n   237→```\n   238→\n   239→## Sections Required\n   240→\n   241→### Summary\n   242→Brief overview of findings.\n   243→\n   244→### Findings\n   245→Numbered list of findings with evidence.\n   246→\n   247→### Recommendations\n   248→Actionable next steps.\n   249→\n   250→## Machine-Readable Markers\n   251→\n   252→At the end of your output, include:\n   253→```\n   254→EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE\n   255→```\n   256→or\n   257→```\n   258→EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: HALT\n   259→```\n   260→\"\"\"\n   261→```\n   262→\n   263→### Key Prompt Rules\n   264→\n   265→1. **Specify output file format** — Tell Claude exactly what frontmatter fields to include\n   266→2. **Require machine-readable markers** — `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE|HALT` for status determination\n   267→3. **Embed input content when small** — Under ~50KB, embed directly in prompt; otherwise use `--file` args\n   268→4. **Be explicit about sections** — Name every required section; don't leave structure to inference\n   269→5. **Include depth/focus constraints** — If the step has configurable depth, encode it in the prompt\n   270→\n   271→### Input Embedding Pattern\n   272→\n   273→```python\n   274→def _embed_inputs(prompt: str, inputs: list[Path], config: MyConfig) -> tuple[str, list[str]]:\n   275→    \"\"\"Embed small input files directly into prompt, return file args for large ones.\"\"\"\n   276→    file_args = []\n   277→    for input_path in inputs:\n   278→        full_path = config.work_dir / input_path\n   279→        if full_path.stat().st_size < 50_000:\n   280→            content = full_path.read_text()\n   281→            prompt += f\"\\n\\n---\\n## Input: {input_path}\\n\\n{content}\"\n   282→        else:\n   283→            file_args.append(str(full_path))\n   284→    return prompt, file_args\n   285→```\n   286→\n   287→## Executor Design Patterns\n   288→\n   289→### Sprint-Style Supervisor Loop\n   290→\n   291→The sprint executor pattern provides supervised execution with monitoring. IMPORTANT: Sprint uses synchronous execution with `threading` for parallelism, NOT `async/await`. Use `time.sleep()` polling loops and `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch dispatch.\n   292→\n   293→```python\n   294→def execute_pipeline(config: MyConfig) -> MyResult:\n   295→    # 1. Pre-flight checks\n   296→    verify_claude_binary()\n   297→\n   298→    # 2. Setup infrastructure\n   299→    signal_handler = SignalHandler()\n   300→    logger = MyLogger(config)\n   301→    tui = MyTUI(config)\n   302→    monitor = OutputMonitor()\n   303→    result = MyResult(config=config)\n   304→    ledger = TurnLedger(initial_budget=config.max_turns)\n   305→\n   306→    # 3. Start TUI\n   307→    tui.start()\n   308→\n   309→    try:\n   310→        for step in config.steps:\n   311→            if signal_handler.shutdown_requested:\n   312→                break\n   313→\n   314→            # 4. Reset monitor for this step\n   315→            monitor.reset(config.output_file(step))\n   316→            monitor.start()\n   317→\n   318→            # 5. Launch Claude subprocess\n   319→            process = ClaudeProcess(config, step)\n   320→            process.start()\n   321→\n   322→            # 6. Supervision loop\n   323→            while process.is_running():\n   324→                if signal_handler.shutdown_requested:\n   325→                    process.stop()\n   326→                    break\n   327→\n   328→                state = monitor.get_state()\n   329→\n   330→                # Stall detection\n   331→                if state.stall_seconds > config.stall_timeout:\n   332→                    process.stop()\n   333→                    break\n   334→\n   335→                # TUI update\n   336→                tui.update(step, state)\n   337→                time.sleep(0.5)\n   338→\n   339→            # 7. Classify result\n   340→            monitor.stop()\n   341→            status = determine_status(process.exit_code, step, config)\n   342→\n   343→            # 8. Record and decide\n   344→            step_result = build_result(step, status, monitor.state)\n   345→            result.add(step_result)\n   346→            logger.write_step_result(step_result)\n   347→\n   348→            if status not in (PASS, PASS_NO_SIGNAL):\n   349→                # Collect diagnostics and halt\n   350→                diagnostics = collect_diagnostics(step_result, monitor.state)\n   351→                write_diagnostic_report(diagnostics, config)\n   352→                result.outcome = HALTED\n   353→                break\n   354→\n   355→    finally:\n   356→        tui.stop()\n   357→        monitor.stop()\n   358→        signal_handler.restore()\n   359→\n   360→    return result\n   361→```\n   362→\n   363→### Status Classification\n   364→\n   365→Map every possible exit condition to a deterministic status:\n   366→\n   367→```python\n   368→def determine_status(exit_code, step, config):\n   369→    # 1. Hard failures\n   370→    if exit_code == 124:\n   371→        return TIMEOUT\n   372→    if exit_code != 0:\n   373→        return ERROR\n   374→\n   375→    # 2. Check for structured result file\n   376→    result_path = config.result_file(step)\n   377→    if result_path.exists():\n   378→        content = result_path.read_text()\n   379→        if \"EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: HALT\" in content:\n   380→            return HALT\n   381→        if \"EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE\" in content:\n   382→            return PASS\n   383→        return PASS_NO_SIGNAL\n   384→\n   385→    # 3. Check for output without result\n   386→    output_path = config.output_file(step)\n   387→    if output_path.exists():\n   388→        if detect_error_max_turns(output_path):\n   389→            return INCOMPLETE\n   390→        return PASS_NO_REPORT\n   391→\n   392→    # 4. No output at all\n   393→    return ERROR\n   394→```\n   395→\n   396→## Integration Patterns\n   397→\n   398→### main.py Registration\n   399→\n   400→```python"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_013s7Jq6Ktd2k1fHEbqKDfny","session_id":"2a0dcd71-e2d6-492b-be01-9ec2d4de516f","uuid":"38c33da6-deb2-4e52-96bc-9f30d4c37864"}

`TurnLedger` to manage budget:\n   431→\n   432→```python\n   433→from superclaude.cli.sprint.models import TurnLedger\n   434→\n   435→@dataclass\n   436→class MyConfig(PipelineConfig):\n   437→ completes, count actual turns used:\n   455→actual_turns = count_turns_from_output(output_path)\n   456→ledger.debit(actual_turns)\n   457→\n   458→# If step used fewer turns than allocated, credit child Claude sessions, use the 4-layer isolation model:\n   466→\n   467→```python\n   468→def setup_isolation(work_dir: Path) -> dict[str, str]:\n   469→    \"\"\"Create isolated environment for child Claude subprocess.\"\"\"\n   470→    env = os.environ.copy()\n   471→\n   472→    # Layer 1: Scoped work directory\n   473→    env[\"CLAUDE_WORK_DIR\"] = str(work_dir)\n   474→\n   475→    # build_step_prompt_with_context(step, prior_results: list[StepResult]) -> str:\n   497→    \"\"\"Build prompt with prior step context injected.\"\"\"\n   498→    base_prompt = step.prompt\n   499→\n   500→    if not prior_results:\n   501→        return base_prompt\n   502→\n   503→    context_block = \"## Prior Step Context\\n\\n\"\n   504→    for result in prior_results[-3:]:  # Keep recent, This skill handles **execution only** for STANDARD and STRICT tier tasks.\\n>\\n> If for any reason no classification header was emitted before this skill was invoked, emit one now using ONLY the tier values STRICT, STANDARD, LIGHT, or EXEMPT (no other values are valid).\\n\\n# /sc:task-unified - Unified Task Execution with Compliance\\n\\n## Purpose\\n\\nUnified command that merges orchestration capabilities with MCP compliance enforcement. Automatically classifies tasks into compliance tiers and enforces appropriate verification.\\n\\n**Key Benefits**:\\n- Single command replaces confusion between `sc:task` and `sc:task-mcp`\\n- Automatic tier classification with confidence scoring\\n- Appropriate verification for each task type\\n- Prevents over-engineering for trivial changes\\n- Ensures safety for critical changes\\n\\n## Triggers\\n\\nUse `/sc:task` when:\\n- Task involves code modifications with downstream impacts\\n- Complexity score >0.6 with code modifications\\n- Multi-file scope (>2 estimated affected files)\\n- Security domain paths detected (auth/, security/, crypto/)\\n- Refactoring or system-wide changes requested\\n\\n**Auto-Suggest Keywords**:\\n- High confidence: \\\"implement feature\\\", \\\"refactor system\\\", \\\"fix security\\\", \\\"add authentication\\\", \\\"update database schema\\\"\\n- Moderate confidence: \\\"add new\\\", \\\"create component\\\", \\\"update service\\\", \\\"modify API\\\"\\n\\n## Usage\\n\\n```bash\\n/sc:task [description]                           # Auto-detect all dimensions\\n/sc:task [description] --compliance strict       # Force STRICT tier\\n/sc:task [description] --compliance light        # Force LIGHT tier\\n/sc:task [description] --skip-compliance         # Bypass compliance (escape hatch)\\n/sc:task [description] --verify auto             # Auto-select verification\\n```\\n\\n## Behavioral Flow\\n\\n### 0. Classification (Already Performed)\\n\\nClassification was handled by the `/sc:task` command before this skill was invoked. The tier has been determined and the classification header has been emitted. Proceed directly to execution based on the classified tier.\\n\\n**Reference** — the tier keyword tables (for context only, do not re-classify):\\n- **STRICT**: security, authentication, database, migration, refactor, breaking change, encrypt, token, session, oauth\\n- **EXEMPT**: explain, search, commit, push, plan, discuss, brainstorm\\n- **LIGHT**: typo, comment, whitespace, lint, docstring, formatting, minor\\n- **STANDARD**: implement, add, create, update, fix, build, modify, change (default)\\n\\n### 2. Confidence Display (Human-Readable)\\n\\nAfter the mandatory classification header, show a human-readable summary:\\n\\n```\\n**Tier: STANDARD** [████████░░] 80%\\n\\nClassified as STANDARD:\\n- Keywords matched: add, implement\\n- Confidence score: 0.78\\n- Considered alternatives: STRICT (0.35)\\n```\\n\\nIf confidence <70%, add prompt: \\\"⚠️ Low confidence. Override with: `--compliance [strict|standard|light|exempt]`\\\"\\n\\n**Note**: The machine-readable header (Section 0) is for telemetry/A/B testing. This human-readable display is for user understanding.\\n\\n### 3. Execution Phase\\n\\nExecute task according to tier requirements:\\n\\n**STRICT Execution**:\\n1. Activate project (mcp__serena__activate_project)\\n2. Verify git working directory clean (git status)\\n3. Load codebase context (codebase-retrieval)\\n4. Check relevant memories (list_memories -> read_memory)\\n5. Identify all affected files and test files\\n6. Make changes with full checklist\\n7. Identify all files that import changed code\\n8. Update all affected files\\n9. Spawn verification agent (quality-engineer)\\n10. Run comprehensive tests: `pytest [path] -v`\\n11. Answer adversarial questions\\n\\n**STANDARD Execution**:\\n1. Load context via codebase-retrieval\\n2. Search downstream impacts (find_referencing_symbols OR grep)\\n3. Make changes\\n4. Run affected tests OR document manual verification\\n5. Verify basic functionality\\n\\n**LIGHT Execution**:\\n1. Quick scope check (files/lines within bounds)\\n2. Make changes\\n3. Quick sanity check (syntax valid, no obvious errors)\\n4. Proceed with judgment\\n\\n**EXEMPT Execution**:\\n1. Execute immediately\\n2. No verification overhead\\n\\n### 4. Verification Phase\\n\\nRoute to appropriate verification based on tier and paths:\\n\\n| Compliance Tier | Verification Method | Token Cost | Timeout |\\n|-----------------|---------------------|------------|---------|\\n| STRICT | Sub-agent (quality-engineer) | 3-5K | 60s |\\n| STANDARD | Direct test execution | 300-500 | 30s |\\n| LIGHT | Skip verification | 0 | 0s |\\n| EXEMPT | Skip verification | 0 | 0s |\\n\\n**Critical Path Override**: Paths matching `auth/`, `security/`, `crypto/`, `models/`, `migrations/` always trigger CRITICAL verification regardless of compliance tier.\\n\\n**Trivial Path Override**: Paths matching `*.md`, `docs/`, `*test*.py` may skip verification.\\n\\n### 5. Feedback Collection\\n\\nAfter completion, collect implicit feedback:\\n- Track if user overrode tier (implicit classification feedback)\\n- Note smooth completion vs errors (quality signal)\\n- Store for calibration learning\\n\\n## MCP Integration\\n\\n**Required Servers by Tier**:\\n- STRICT: Sequential, Serena (fallback not allowed)\\n- STANDARD: Sequential, Context7 (fallback allowed)\\n- LIGHT: None required (fallback allowed)\\n- EXEMPT: None required\\n\\n**Circuit Breaker Behavior**:\\n- If required servers unavailable for STRICT tier, block task execution\\n- For other tiers, use fallbacks with noted limitations\\n\\n## Tool Coordination\\n\\n**Planning Phase**:\\n1. TodoWrite: Create task breakdown\\n2. codebase-retrieval: Load context\\n3. list_memories / read_memory: Check project state\\n\\n**Execution Phase**:\\n1. Edit/MultiEdit/Write: Make changes\\n2. Grep/Glob: Find references\\n3. find_referencing_symbols: Trace dependencies\\n\\n**Verification Phase**:\\n1. Task (quality-engineer): Spawn verification agent (STRICT only)\\n2. Bash: Run tests directly (STANDARD)\\n3. think_about_task_adherence: Reflect on completeness\\n\\n**Completion Phase**:\\n1. write_memory: Save session state\\n2. think_about_whether_you_are_done: Final check\\n\\n## Examples\\n\\n### STRICT Task\\n```\\n/sc:task \\\"implement user authentication with JWT\\\"\\n\\n-> Classified as STRICT (security domain, authentication keyword)\\n-> Full 6-category checklist enforced\\n-> Verification agent spawned\\n-> Adversarial questions answered\\n```\\n\\n### STANDARD Task\\n```\\n/sc:task \\\"add pagination to user list\\\"\\n\\n-> Classified as STANDARD (add keyword, typical feature)\\n-> Context loaded before editing\\n-> Downstream impacts checked\\n-> Direct test execution\\n```\\n\\n### LIGHT Task\\n```\\n/sc:task \\\"fix typo in README\\\"\\n\\n-> Classified as LIGHT (trivial keyword, documentation path)\\n-> Quick sanity check only\\n-> No verification delay\\n```\\n\\n### EXEMPT Task\\n```\\n/sc:task \\\"explain how the auth flow works\\\"\\n\\n-> Classified as EXEMPT (explain pattern detected)\\n-> Immediate execution\\n-> No compliance overhead\\n```\\n\\n### Override Example\\n```\\n/sc:task \\\"update config file\\\" --compliance strict\\n\\n-> User override to STRICT regardless of classification\\n-> Full verification enforced\\n```\\n\\n## Boundaries\\n\\n**Will:**\\n- Classify tasks into appropriate compliance tiers\\n- Enforce tier-appropriate verification requirements\\n- Provide confidence scoring with rationale\\n- Track feedback for continuous calibration\\n- Support user overrides with justification\\n\\n**Will Not:**\\n- Skip safety-critical verification for STRICT tasks\\n- Apply STRICT overhead to genuinely trivial changes\\n- Override user's explicit compliance choice\\n- Script\n\n**Task**: T01.02\n**Roadmap Items**: R-006, R-007\n**Date**: 2026-03-08\n\n## Script Location\n\n`scripts/check-ref-staleness.py`\n\n## Purpose\n\nCompares field names in ref files (`pipeline-spec.md`, `code-templates.md`) against the live Python API in `models.py` and `gates.py`. Exits non-zero on any mismatch.\n\n## Detection Capabilities\n\n1. **GateCriteria field name drift**: Detects old `tier=` vs correct `enforcement_tier=`\n2. **Frontmatter field name drift**: Detects `required_frontmatter=` vs correct `required_frontmatter_fields=`\n3. **Tier casing errors**: Flags lowercase tier values that should be UPPER_CASE\n4. **SemanticCheck field drift**: Detects `fn=` vs correct `check_fn=`\n5. **Signature drift**: Detects `tuple[bool, str]` return type vs correct `Callable[[str], bool]`\n\n## Checked Locations\n\n- `src/superclaude/skills/sc-cli-portify/refs/pipeline-spec.md`\n- `src/superclaude/skills/sc-cli-portify/refs/code-templates.md`\n- `src/superclaude/skills/sc-cli-portify-protocol/refs/pipeline-spec.md` (if exists)\n- `src/superclaude/skills/sc-cli-portify-protocol/refs/code-templates.md` (if exists)\n\n## Usage\n\n```bash\nuv run python scripts/check-ref-staleness.py\n```\n\nExit code 0 = all refs match live API. Exit code 1 = mismatches found.\n\n## Reusability\n\nDesigned for reuse in Phase 4 (M4.1) conformance checking and CI integration.\n```\n\n###
